problems.pattern_explanation: State machine DP with three states—held (holding stock), sold (just sold, in cooldown), rest (not holding, not cooling). Transitions encode the cooldown constraint explicitly.
problems.trigger_phrase: Stock trading with cooldown—state machine DP with held/sold/rest states. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: held = max(prev_held, prev_rest - price); sold = prev_held + price; rest = max(prev_rest, prev_sold). Initialise held=-prices[0], sold=rest=0. Return max(sold, rest). Time: **O(n)**. Space: **O(1)**.

problems.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Transaction Fee
problems.problem_statement: Maximise profit with unlimited transactions but a fee per transaction.
problems.pattern_explanation: Two-state DP: cash (not holding) and hold (holding). On each day, cash = max(cash, hold + price - fee); hold = max(hold, cash - price). The fee is subtracted on sell.
problems.trigger_phrase: Stock unlimited transactions with fee—two-state DP: cash and hold, fee deducted on sell. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: cash=0, hold=-prices[0]. For each price: new_cash = max(cash, hold+price-fee); new_hold = max(hold, cash-price). Update. Return cash. Time: **O(n)**. Space: **O(1)**.

problems.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
problems.problem_statement: Find the maximum profit with at most 2 transactions.
problems.pattern_explanation: Track four state variables: buy1, sell1, buy2, sell2. Each represents the optimal value after that particular action. Transitions update all four in a single pass.
problems.trigger_phrase: At most k=2 stock transactions—track state per transaction in one pass. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: buy1 = buy2 = -inf; sell1 = sell2 = 0. For each price: buy1 = max(buy1, -price); sell1 = max(sell1, buy1+price); buy2 = max(buy2, sell1-price); sell2 = max(sell2, buy2+price). Return sell2. Time: **O(n)**. Space: **O(1)**.

problems.title: Maximum Profit in Job Scheduling
problems.problem_statement: Schedule non-overlapping jobs (with start time, end time, profit) to maximise total profit.
problems.pattern_explanation: Sort jobs by end time. dp[i] = max profit using only the first i jobs. For each job, binary search for the latest non-overlapping job. Transition: max(skip, profit[i] + dp[prev_job]).
problems.trigger_phrase: Maximum profit non-overlapping intervals—sort by end, DP with binary search for latest compatible job. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: Sort by end time. dp = [(0,0)]. For each job (start, end, profit): bisect_right on end times to find latest compatible. new_profit = dp[idx][1] + profit. If > dp[-1][1], append (end, new_profit). Time: **O(n log n)**. Space: **O(n)**.

problems.title: Longest Increasing Path in a Matrix
problems.problem_statement: Find the length of the longest strictly increasing path in a matrix, moving in 4 directions.
problems.pattern_explanation: DFS with memoization on each cell. dp[i][j] = longest increasing path starting at (i,j). Since paths are strictly increasing (forming a DAG), no cycle can exist and memoization is valid.
problems.trigger_phrase: Longest increasing path in grid—DFS with memoization; strictly increasing ensures DAG, no cycle. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: For each cell, memoized DFS: try all 4 directions where neighbor > current. dp[i][j] = 1 + max(dp of valid neighbors). Return global max. Time: **O(m*n)**. Space: **O(m*n)**.

problems.title: Number of Islands II
problems.problem_statement: Given a sequence of land-adding operations on a grid, return the number of islands after each operation.
problems.pattern_explanation: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) processes dynamic connectivity efficiently. Each land addition is O(alpha) amortized with path compression. The island count decrements when merging distinct components.
problems.trigger_phrase: Dynamic island formation with connectivity tracking—Union-Find with component count. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: Union-Find over grid cells. On each land addition: increment island count. For each land neighbor, if different component, union them and decrement count. Record count after each step. Time: **O(k * alpha(m*n))** for k operations. Space: **O(m*n)**.

problems.title: Word Search II
problems.problem_statement: Find all words from a dictionary that exist in a board (adjacent cells, no reuse per path).
problems.pattern_explanation: Build a Trie of all words. DFS with backtracking on the board; prune branches not in the Trie. This avoids restarting for each word and processes all words simultaneously.
problems.trigger_phrase: Find multiple words on a grid—Trie + DFS with backtracking for simultaneous multi-word search. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: Insert all words into a Trie. DFS from each cell, traversing Trie nodes simultaneously. When a Trie node marks end-of-word, record the word. Backtrack by restoring cell. Prune Trie nodes after finding words. Time: **O(M*N*4^L)** where L=max word length. Space: **O(total word chars)**.

problems.title: Number of Distinct Islands
problems.problem_statement: Count the number of distinct island shapes (islands that would overlap if translated).
problems.pattern_explanation: DFS each island and record the path signature relative to the starting cell. Use a set of these relative-position tuples to deduplicate shapes.
problems.trigger_phrase: Distinct island shapes—DFS with relative coordinates stored as canonical signature. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: DFS on each unvisited '1'. Record (r - start_r, c - start_c) and direction for each step. Hash the resulting tuple as the island signature. Add to a set. Return set size. Time: **O(rows*cols)**. Space: **O(rows*cols)**.

problems.title: Largest Submatrix With Rearrangements
problems.problem_statement: Find the largest area rectangle in a binary matrix if you can rearrange columns in any order.
problems.pattern_explanation: Compute column heights (consecutive 1s ending at each row). For each row, sort heights in descending order. The area at rank k is heights[k] * (k+1). No need for a full histogram algorithm.
problems.trigger_phrase: Largest rearrangeable rectangle in binary matrix—column heights, sort per row, width = rank. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: Build column heights (reset to 0 on '0'). For each row, sort heights descending. Area candidate at index k is heights[k] * (k+1). Track maximum. Time: **O(m*n log n)**. Space: **O(n)**.

problems.title: Find Right Interval
problems.problem_statement: For each interval, find the minimum start point of another interval >= the current interval's end.
problems.pattern_explanation: Sort intervals by start. For each interval's end, binary search for the smallest start >= end. A sorted map of start->index enables this in O(log n).
problems.trigger_phrase: Next interval start >= current end—sort starts, binary search per query. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: Build sorted array of (start, index) pairs. For each interval's end, binary search for smallest start >= end. Record result index or -1. Time: **O(n log n)**. Space: **O(n)**.

problems.title: Minimum Interval to Include Each Query
problems.problem_statement: For each query point, find the minimum length interval that contains it.
problems.pattern_explanation: Sort queries offline. Sort intervals by start. Sweep: add all intervals starting before or at the current query to a min-heap keyed by length. Pop intervals ending before the query. Heap top is the answer.
problems.trigger_phrase: Offline query: minimum interval containing each point—sort both, sweep with min-heap of active intervals. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: Sort queries with original indices. Sort intervals by start. Min-heap keyed by (length, end). For each query, add all intervals with start <= query; pop expired intervals with end < query. Heap top gives answer. Time: **O((n+q) log n)**. Space: **O(n+q)**.

problems.title: Find K-th Smallest Pair Distance
problems.problem_statement: Find the k-th smallest distance among all pairs in an array.
problems.pattern_explanation: Binary search on the answer. The feasibility function counts pairs with distance <= mid using a sliding window on the sorted array. This converts an order-statistics problem into binary search.
problems.trigger_phrase: K-th smallest pair distance—binary search the distance, count pairs with sliding window. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: Sort array. Binary search on distance in [0, max-min]. Count pairs with distance <= mid using two pointers. If count >= k, search lower; else search higher. Time: **O(n log n + n log(max-min))**. Space: **O(1)**.

problems.title: Summary Ranges
problems.problem_statement: Given a sorted unique integer array, return the smallest sorted list of ranges that cover all numbers.
problems.pattern_explanation: Linear scan tracking the start of the current range. When a gap appears (nums[i] != nums[i-1]+1), close the current range and start a new one.
problems.trigger_phrase: Compress consecutive integers into ranges—scan tracking range start, close on gap. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: Track start index. Scan: if nums[i] != nums[i-1]+1, append range from nums[start] to nums[i-1], reset start. Append last range. Time: **O(n)**. Space: **O(1)** extra.

problems.title: Arithmetic Slices
problems.problem_statement: Count the number of arithmetic subarrays of length at least 3.
problems.pattern_explanation: DP: dp[i] = number of arithmetic subarrays ending at i. If the difference matches, dp[i] = dp[i-1] + 1. This counts all valid endings at i efficiently.
problems.trigger_phrase: Count arithmetic subarrays—DP extending count when common difference matches. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: dp[i] = 0. For i >= 2: if nums[i]-nums[i-1] == nums[i-1]-nums[i-2], dp[i] = dp[i-1] + 1. Add dp[i] to total. Return total. Time: **O(n)**. Space: **O(1)**.

problems.title: Number of Arithmetic Triplets
problems.problem_statement: Count triplets (i, j, k) with i < j < k in a strictly increasing array where nums[j]-nums[i] == nums[k]-nums[j] == diff.
problems.pattern_explanation: For each element x, check if x-diff and x-2*diff are in a hashset. A single pass with a growing set suffices because the array is strictly increasing.
problems.trigger_phrase: Arithmetic triplet count in sorted array—hashset check for two predecessors per element. The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: For each nums[i], if nums[i]-diff and nums[i]-2*diff are both in a set, increment count. Add nums[i] to set. Time: **O(n)**. Space: **O(n)**.

problems.title: Number of Subsequences That Satisfy the Given Sum Condition
problems.problem_statement: Count subsequences where min + max <= target.
problems.pattern_explanation: Sort the array. Two pointers: for each left (minimum), find the rightmost right (maximum) where nums[left] + nums[right] <= target. All 2^(right-left) subsequences with that min are valid.
problems.trigger_phrase: Count subsequences with min+max bound after sorting—two pointers count valid subsequences in O(1). The mental move is: ignore the story, isolate the structure, and ask which standard pattern makes repeated work disappear.
problems.detailed_solution: Sort. Precompute powers of 2 mod 1e9+7. Two pointers l=0, r=n-1. If nums[l]+nums[r] <= target, add pow2[r-l] and advance l. Else retreat r. Time: **O(n log n)**. Space: **O(n)**.
